primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected cutaneous malignant melanoma of the upper back, ulcerated and bleeding

pathophysiology_summary: A pre-existing junctional/compound naevus has undergone malignant transformation to invasive cutaneous melanoma, driven by cumulative UV damage on a background of severe childhood sunburns. Radial and vertical growth-phase proliferation of atypical melanocytes produces the classic ABCDE features (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution) and the 'ugly duckling' morphology. Vertical growth with surface ulceration explains the spontaneous bleeding and pruritus and is an adverse prognostic sign (ulceration upstages the AJCC T category). Definitive diagnosis and Breslow-thickness staging are HISTOLOGICAL and require complete excisional biopsy — they cannot be established remotely, by history, by photograph, or even by dermoscopy alone. Regional nodal status cannot be assessed via telehealth. The immediate clinical issues are (1) recognising the lesion as high-suspicion skin malignancy requiring urgent excision, and (2) controlling the minor surface bleeding, which is a mechanical consequence of the fragile ulcerated tumour surface and is manageable with direct pressure.

differential.evidence_basis: A long-standing mole that has rapidly changed and spontaneously bled is melanoma until proven otherwise — even before structured questioning.
Seborrhoeic keratoses are common, can be dark, and bleed when knocked — but do not usually arise from a flat freckle or evolve this rapidly.
Another skin malignancy that can bleed; still needs excision, but typically grows slowly over months to years rather than weeks.
A rapidly growing, easily-bleeding vascular lesion — but is usually bright red, not variegated black/grey/brown, and does not arise from a pigmented naevus.
Can be irregular and multi-coloured, but spontaneous bleeding and rapid evolution push strongly towards invasive malignancy over a simple dysplastic naevus.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even on the opening complaint alone, melanoma must be the leading concern and the AI Doctor must NOT reassure the patient that a changing, bleeding mole is a benign freckle.

differential.evidence_basis: ABCDE fully met (asymmetry, irregular border, colour variegation, 8-9 mm diameter, 8-week evolution) plus the ugly-duckling sign, new ulceration/bleeding, and severe childhood sunburn history.
Remains on the list as an alternative skin cancer requiring excision, but the rapid evolution favours melanoma.
Lower probability given evolution from a flat freckle, ugly-duckling morphology and colour variegation, but cannot be excluded without histology.
Still possible but demoted by the bleeding and rapid change.
Pigmented/variegated appearance arising from an evolving naevus is against a purely vascular pyogenic granuloma.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the full ABCDE + ugly-duckling + sunburn history is elicited, melanoma should be firmly leading and the benign options substantially downgraded. Failure to shift is anchoring on 'it's just a mole'.

differential.evidence_basis: High-suspicion pigmented lesion that CANNOT be confirmed or excluded remotely — the correct telehealth endpoint is urgent in-person assessment and excisional biopsy, not a remote diagnosis.
The other malignancy that the excisional biopsy will also address; management pathway (urgent excision) is the same.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final position for a telehealth consult is: high suspicion of skin malignancy, cannot be diagnosed remotely, needs urgent in-person excision/biopsy. A confident remote diagnosis (either 'it's melanoma' or 'it's benign') is wrong.

red_flags.description: Rapidly evolving pigmented lesion — increased diameter, elevation and colour change over ~8 weeks (the 'E' of ABCDE and ugly-duckling sign)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rapid change in a melanocytic lesion is the single most important historical red flag for melanoma and mandates excisional biopsy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Lesion dismissed as a stable benign mole; melanoma progresses, deepens (increasing Breslow thickness) and may metastasise before diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Spontaneous bleeding and surface ulceration of a pigmented lesion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ulceration/bleeding indicates vertical growth-phase / surface breakdown of the tumour — an adverse prognostic feature that upstages melanoma; also the acute symptom needing haemostasis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Interpreted as trivial trauma; both the malignancy and ongoing bleeding under-managed.

red_flags.description: Ugly-duckling sign — the lesion is morphologically distinct from the patient's other naevi
red_flags.clinical_significance: A lesion that stands out from a person's other moles has a high positive predictive value for melanoma and should prompt referral even if individual ABCDE features are borderline.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The discriminating gestalt feature is not elicited and the lesion is under-triaged.

red_flags.description: Irregular/notched border with colour variegation (black, brown, grey, erythematous rim)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Border irregularity and multiple colours are core ABCDE criteria strongly associated with melanoma.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Morphological red flags not documented; suspicion inappropriately lowered.

red_flags.description: Regional (axillary/cervical) lymphadenopathy indicating possible nodal spread
red_flags.clinical_significance: Palpable regional nodes would raise concern for metastatic spread and change staging/urgency — but nodal basins cannot be palpated over a telehealth chat and must be assessed in person.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: False reassurance if the AI Doctor implies nodal status can be cleared remotely.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'it's just a mole that caught on the shirt' and reassuring the patient rather than recognising a changing, bleeding, ugly-duckling lesion as high-suspicion melanoma.

Attempting to diagnose (or exclude) melanoma from a chat description or photograph — telehealth cannot substitute for dermoscopy and histology; the correct output is urgent in-person biopsy, not a remote verdict.

Advising cryotherapy, shave excision, curettage, cautery or a topical agent for a pigmented lesion — this destroys the tissue needed for histological staging (Breslow thickness) and is a management error.
Treating a small, controllable surface bleed as a haemorrhagic emergency and calling 000 — over-escalation; the correct pathway is direct pressure plus an URGENT (not emergency) skin-cancer referral.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Dermoscopic assessment of the pigment network, vascular pattern and asymmetry
Histological diagnosis and Breslow thickness (requires excisional biopsy)
Palpation of regional axillary and cervical lymph node basins
Objective vital signs / degree of any blood loss without a home device
Reliable measurement of true lesion diameter and depth
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the lesion as high-suspicion skin malignancy and arrange an URGENT in-person review with a skin-cancer clinic / dermatologist / general surgeon for complete excisional biopsy (within ~1-2 weeks); confirm haemostasis via patient-applied direct pressure and a protective dressing in the meantime; escalate to ED only if bleeding is uncontrolled.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient that a bleeding, rapidly-changing pigmented lesion is a benign mole — or, conversely, delivering a confident remote 'melanoma' verdict — both misuse telehealth. The specific harm of false reassurance is delayed excision, increased Breslow thickness and metastatic progression of a curable-when-early cancer.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Cancer Council Australia: Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Melanoma 2018
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A suspicious pigmented lesion should undergo complete excisional biopsy with a 2 mm margin for histological diagnosis and Breslow staging; partial/shave biopsy and destructive treatments are discouraged for suspected melanoma.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP: early detection of skin cancer / MBS skin cancer services guidance
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Lesions with ABCDE features or the ugly-duckling sign, and any pigmented lesion that is changing or bleeding, warrant urgent assessment and excision rather than watchful waiting.
